_meta.description: Primary food database for Indian staples. Searched before USDA.
_meta.inclusion_rule: Items are included only if (a) confidence is 'high' from IFCT 2017, OR (b) confidence is 'medium' AND the food is Indian-specific or not reliably covered by USDA. Low-confidence items and globally-common foods with strong USDA coverage are excluded — USDA is the fallback for those (e.g. broccoli, kiwi, quinoa, avocado, chia seeds).

confidence_notes.high: Macros and micros from IFCT 2017 directly
confidence_notes.medium: Macros from IFCT, micros from USDA/WHO cross-reference — retained only because food is Indian-specific or absent from USDA

items.name: Moong dal, yellow split, raw
items.notes: Raw uncooked split moong. Green whole moong has more fiber.

items.name: Moong dal, green whole, raw
items.notes: Whole green mung. Higher fiber and calcium than split version.

items.name: Moong dal, sprouted
items.notes: Sprouted moong is ~90% water. Vitamin C increases significantly on sprouting.

items.name: Masoor dal, red split, raw
items.notes: One of the highest-protein dals.

items.name: Masoor dal, whole brown, raw
items.notes: More fiber than split version due to retained husk.

items.name: Toor dal, split, raw
items.notes: Most commonly used dal in Indian cooking.

items.name: Chana dal, split, raw
items.notes: Higher fat content than other dals.

items.name: Urad dal, white split, raw
items.notes: High calcium and iron.

items.name: Urad dal, black whole, raw
items.notes: Whole black urad with husk. Used for dal makhani.

items.name: Moong dal, cooked
items.notes: Indian-specific — retained despite medium confidence. Plain cooked, no oil or spices. Values vary with water absorption ratio.

items.name: Toor dal, cooked
items.notes: Indian-specific — retained despite medium confidence. Plain cooked toor dal without tempering.

items.name: Masoor dal, cooked
items.notes: Indian-specific — retained despite medium confidence.

items.name: Kala chana, raw
items.notes: Higher calcium than kabuli variety due to retained husk.

items.name: Chickpeas, cooked
items.notes: Indian-specific — retained despite medium confidence. Plain boiled, no oil or spices.

items.name: Rajma, cooked
items.notes: Indian-specific — retained despite medium confidence.

items.name: Rice, white, raw
items.notes: Macros roughly halve when cooked due to water absorption.

items.name: Rice, white, cooked
items.notes: Plain cooked white rice, no salt or fat added.

items.name: Rice, brown, raw
items.notes: Significantly more magnesium and zinc than white rice.

items.name: Oats, rolled, raw
items.notes: High in beta-glucan fiber.

items.name: Oats, cooked
items.notes: Values drop significantly from raw due to water absorption.

items.name: Jowar, raw
items.notes: Indian-specific grain — retained despite medium confidence. Magnesium value not available in IFCT for this entry.

items.name: Bajra, raw
items.notes: One of the best grain sources of iron.

items.name: Ragi, raw
items.notes: Best grain source of calcium in India at 344mg/100g.

items.name: Poha, raw
items.notes: Very high iron (20mg) due to processing. Bioavailability is moderate.

items.name: Vermicelli, dry
items.notes: Indian-specific preparation — retained despite medium confidence. Values vary by brand.

items.name: Atta, whole wheat flour
items.notes: Stone-ground whole wheat flour used for chapati and roti.

items.name: Maida, refined wheat flour
items.notes: Much lower fiber and micronutrients than whole wheat.

items.name: Besan, chickpea flour
items.notes: High protein flour.

items.name: Ragi flour
items.notes: Same values as whole ragi grain. Very high calcium.

items.name: Corn flour, refined
items.notes: Pure starch. Negligible protein and micronutrients.

items.name: Spinach, raw
items.notes: Excellent source of Vitamin A and iron.

items.name: Sweet potato, raw
items.notes: Very high Vitamin A. Orange-fleshed variety assumed.

items.name: Carrot, raw
items.notes: Excellent Vitamin A source.

items.name: Bitter gourd, raw
items.notes: Very high Vitamin C.

items.name: Capsicum, red, raw
items.notes: Much higher Vitamin A and C than green capsicum.

items.name: Yellow capsicum, raw
items.notes: Exceptionally high Vitamin C — nearly double red capsicum. Values from USDA approximate for yellow bell pepper raw per 100g.

items.name: Garlic, raw
items.notes: Used in small quantities typically.

items.name: Drumstick, raw
items.notes: Very high Vitamin C and fiber.

items.name: Banana, raw green
items.notes: Indian-specific use — retained despite medium confidence. More resistant starch and less sugar than ripe.

items.name: Mango, raw green
items.notes: Higher Vitamin C than ripe mango. Used in chutneys and pickles.

items.name: Papaya, raw green
items.notes: Indian-specific use — retained despite medium confidence. Used as vegetable in Indian cooking.

items.name: Guava
items.notes: One of the best fruit sources of Vitamin C.

items.name: Jackfruit, raw green
items.notes: Indian-specific — retained despite medium confidence. Used as meat substitute in Indian cooking.

items.name: Coconut, fresh
items.notes: Fresh coconut flesh. High saturated fat.

items.name: Milk, whole cow
items.notes: Good source of calcium, B12 and Vitamin D.

items.name: Milk, buffalo
items.notes: Higher fat and calcium than cow milk. Common in North India.

items.name: Paneer
items.notes: Excellent calcium source. High protein dairy.

items.name: Ghee
items.notes: Pure fat. Notable Vitamin A and some Vitamin D.

items.name: Skimmed milk
items.notes: Vitamin A removed with fat during skimming.

items.name: Khoya
items.notes: Indian-specific — retained despite medium confidence. Not in USDA. Very high calcium.

items.name: Almonds, raw
items.notes: Excellent calcium, magnesium and fiber.

items.name: Cashews, raw
items.notes: High iron and zinc. Higher carbs than most nuts.

items.name: Walnuts
items.notes: Rich in omega-3 (ALA).

items.name: Peanuts, dry roasted
items.notes: Roasting slightly increases calories due to moisture loss.

items.name: Fox nuts, raw
items.notes: Indian-specific — retained despite medium confidence. Very low fat, high fiber.

items.name: Dried figs
items.notes: Good calcium source among dry fruits.

items.name: Dried apricots
items.notes: Very high potassium and good Vitamin A source.

items.name: Sesame seeds
items.notes: Exceptional calcium (975mg) and iron (14.6mg).

items.name: Flaxseeds
items.notes: Highest fiber content among seeds. Rich in omega-3 (ALA).

items.name: Rice bran oil
items.notes: Contains oryzanol — not tracked in standard nutrient panels.

items.name: Coconut oil
items.notes: High in saturated MCTs.

items.name: Strawberries
items.notes: Not in IFCT — values from USDA. High Vitamin C source.

items.name: Kiwi
items.notes: Not in IFCT — values from USDA. Very high Vitamin C.

items.name: Dragon fruit, white
items.notes: Limited data globally. Macros are reasonable estimates; micronutrients approximate.

items.name: Dragon fruit, pink
items.notes: Pink variety has more antioxidants than white. Micronutrients approximate.

items.name: Blueberries
items.notes: Not in IFCT — values from USDA.

items.name: Passion fruit
items.notes: Very high fiber. Good Vitamin A source.

items.name: Pear
items.notes: Not in IFCT — values from USDA.

items.name: Soy milk, unsweetened
items.notes: Unfortified base values. Commercial brands typically add calcium (~120mg), B12, and Vitamin D — check your brand label.

items.name: Almond milk, unsweetened
items.notes: Very low protein compared to dairy or soy milk. Fortified versions have more calcium.

items.name: Oat milk, unsweetened
items.notes: Higher carbs than other plant milks. Often fortified with calcium and B12.

items.name: Coconut milk, full fat
items.notes: Full fat canned variety used in cooking. Much higher in fat and calories than drinking milks.

items.name: Coconut milk, light
items.notes: Lower fat canned version. About 60% less fat than full fat.

items.name: Rice milk, unsweetened
items.notes: Highest carbs, lowest protein of all plant milks. Often fortified.

items.name: Tofu, firm
items.notes: Calcium varies significantly by brand — calcium sulfate-set tofu can have 200–500mg per 100g. Values here are for a standard firm tofu.

items.name: Tofu, silken
items.notes: Lower protein and fat than firm tofu. Higher water content.

items.name: Tempeh
items.notes: Fermented soy. Higher protein than tofu. Trace B12 from fermentation — not a reliable B12 source.

items.name: Edamame, fresh
items.notes: Immature soybeans. Complete protein source. Values for shelled edamame.

items.name: Soybeans, mature, raw
items.notes: Raw dry soybeans. Extremely nutrient-dense. Must be cooked before eating.

items.name: Nutritional yeast
items.notes: B12 value (0.6mcg) is for unfortified yeast. Fortified brands (like Bragg) contain 8–24mcg per serving — check your label. This is a key B12 source for vegans.

items.name: Hemp seeds
items.notes: 700mg magnesium is correct — one of the highest food sources. Complete protein with all essential amino acids.

items.name: Chia seeds
items.notes: Exceptional calcium (631mg) and fiber (34g). Rich in omega-3 ALA.

items.name: Coconut cream
items.notes: Thicker and higher fat than coconut milk. Used for desserts and thick curries.

items.name: Muskmelon
items.notes: Kharbooja. USDA entry: Melons, cantaloupe, raw.

items.name: Cape gooseberry
items.notes: Rasbhari/physalis. Some micronutrients absent from USDA SR Legacy entry — incomplete record.

items.name: Grapes, green
items.notes: Angoor (hari). USDA does not distinguish green/black grapes nutritionally — same values as black grapes entry.

items.name: Grapes, black
items.notes: Angoor (kali). USDA does not distinguish green/black grapes nutritionally — same values as green grapes entry.

items.name: Radish
items.notes: Mooli. USDA entry: Radishes, raw.

items.name: Mustard greens
items.notes: Sarson saag. High in vitamin A and C. USDA entry: Mustard greens, raw.

items.name: Yam
items.notes: Suran/jimikand. USDA entry: Yam, raw.

items.name: Red cabbage
items.notes: Lal patta gobhi. Higher vitamin C and anthocyanins than green cabbage. USDA entry: Cabbage, red, raw.

items.name: Spring onion
items.notes: Hara pyaz. Includes tops and bulb. USDA entry: Onions, spring or scallions, raw.

items.name: Button mushroom
items.notes: Khumb. One of few non-animal sources of vitamin D and B12 (trace). USDA entry: Mushrooms, white, raw.

items.name: Asparagus
items.notes: Shatavari. USDA entry: Asparagus, raw.

items.name: Kale
items.notes: Exceptionally high in vitamin A, vitamin C, and calcium. USDA entry: Kale, raw.

items.name: Broccoli
items.notes: Hari gobhi. High vitamin C. USDA entry: Broccoli, raw.

items.name: Avocado
items.notes: Makhanphal/butter fruit. High in healthy fats and potassium. USDA entry: Avocados, raw, California.

items.name: Jowar flour
items.notes: Jowar atta. USDA entry is refined sorghum flour — fiber may be lower than whole grain jowar flour (IFCT values preferred when available).

items.name: Haricot beans
items.notes: Navy beans/haricot. Different from French beans (haricot vert). USDA entry: Beans, navy, mature seeds, raw.
